title: SEGMENT 18 — The frontier: CO2-fixing E. coli and the path to carbon-neutral biomanufacturing

lines.text: SEGMENT 18 — The frontier: CO2-fixing E. coli and the path to carbon-neutral biomanufacturing

I want to close with the frontier, because the long arc of metabolic engineering — the arc that motivates the whole project — points toward a chemistry that does not depend on extracting carbon from the ground.
Today, almost every microbial production process is fed by sugar, which was made by plants from CO2 and sunlight.
The plants and the sugar processing are between us and the CO2.
The metabolic engineering question becomes: can we cut out the middleman?
Can we engineer microbes that, themselves, take CO2 from the air, fix it into organic molecules, and run the rest of the chemistry on that internally generated carbon?
That sounds like science fiction, but in 2019 a remarkable paper came out of Ron Milo's lab at the Weizmann Institute in Israel that demonstrated it.

The paper, with Shmuel Gleizer as first author, described an engineered strain of E. coli — an organism that is naturally a heterotroph, eating sugars and other organic carbon sources — that had been converted, by metabolic engineering and laboratory evolution, into an autotroph.

The strain used CO2 as its only carbon source, fixing it via the engineered installation of the Calvin-Benson-Bassham cycle — the same pathway plants use for carbon fixation — plus an external energy source from formate, an organic molecule but one that does not contribute carbon to biomass.
Let me unpack that, because it is a stunning piece of work.
The Calvin cycle requires a key enzyme called RuBisCO — ribulose bisphosphate carboxylase — which catalyzes the addition of CO2 onto a five-carbon sugar acceptor.
RuBisCO is famously the most abundant protein on Earth, because plants and algae need so much of it to fix carbon, and it is also famously inefficient and slow.
E. coli does not have RuBisCO.

The Milo team engineered into E. coli a working Calvin cycle by introducing RuBisCO and phosphoribulokinase from cyanobacteria, then knocking out the E. coli enzymes that would have prevented the engineered pathway from running productively.
They also rewired the central carbon metabolism so that the cell would route its precursor demand through the new CO2-fixation entry point rather than through the sugar uptake systems.
Just having all the enzymes in place was not enough.
The engineered strain, on first construction, did not grow on CO2.

The team then subjected the strain to laboratory evolution — a directed evolution at the level of the whole cell — by growing the strain for many generations under conditions of steadily decreasing organic carbon availability and steadily increasing reliance on CO2 fixation.

After about 350 days of evolution, with periodic genome sequencing to track the accumulating mutations, they had a strain that grew autotrophically — using CO2 as its only carbon source — with no organic carbon input beyond formate for energy.
The strain had picked up a handful of beneficial mutations that the rational engineering had missed.
The combination of design and evolution had worked.
That paper was a landmark.
It demonstrated that you could, by metabolic engineering plus directed evolution, take a heterotrophic workhorse and convert it into a CO2-fixing autotroph.

If this can be made efficient enough, and if the energy source can eventually come from renewable electricity rather than from formate-from-natural-gas, you have the foundation for a kind of microbial chemistry that genuinely closes the carbon loop.
CO2 goes in.
Products go out.
No net new fossil carbon is released.
Combined with renewable energy, this is the kind of biomanufacturing that could be part of a credible path to a decarbonized chemical industry.
There are major caveats.
The engineered E. coli in the Milo paper grows slowly compared to wild-type E. coli on glucose.
The energy input from formate is expensive.
The yields of any product on CO2 would be lower than yields on sugar.
The infrastructure for an industrial-scale CO2 fermentation industry does not exist.
None of these problems are fundamental, but all of them are real, and the time horizon for converting this proof of concept into industrial reality is at minimum a couple of decades.
There are parallel approaches worth mentioning.
One-carbon metabolism — engineering organisms to grow on methanol, formate, methane, or syngas instead of sugar — is a closely related research program.
Methanol-utilizing E. coli strains have been engineered by several groups, notably the labs of Greg Stephanopoulos at MIT and Julia Vorholt at ETH Zürich.

Cupriavidus necator (formerly Ralstonia eutropha) is a natural hydrogen-and-CO2-fixing bacterium that has been the subject of substantial engineering effort, including for the production of bioplastics like PHB.
Synechocystis** and other cyanobacteria are being engineered as photoautotrophic production platforms that use sunlight directly to drive CO2 fixation.
The collection of platforms for one-carbon and CO2-based biomanufacturing is growing, and the technology is steadily maturing.

The analogy I want you to carry: **the frontier of metabolic engineering is the rewiring of one-carbon metabolism — engineered E. coli that fix CO2 (Milo lab, 2019), methylotrophic platforms that grow on methanol, photoautotrophic cyanobacteria that use sunlight.

The destination is a truly carbon-neutral biomanufacturing in which the chemical industry is fed by CO2 and renewable energy, not petroleum.** Whether we get there — whether the engineering matures fast enough and the economics shift far enough — is one of the great applied biology questions of the next half century.
Let me close on the long arc.
We started this lecture with a single sentence: metabolic engineering is the rewriting of cellular chemistry so the cell makes what you want.

We have now walked through the workhorses, the founding hits, the Keasling artemisinin story, the methods of pathway discovery, flux balance analysis, directed evolution, cofactor engineering, the multi-locus editing tools, the commercial waves of Amyris and Ginkgo, the plant natural products, sustainable manufacturing, alt-protein, modular cloning, CRISPR, AI-driven design, and now the frontier.

The arc — from one recombinant gene in E. coli producing insulin in 1978 to engineered E. coli fixing CO2 in 2019, with everything in between — is the arc of a discipline maturing from a research curiosity into a foundational industrial technology.
If I had to bet on what the field looks like in 2050, I would say something like this.

By 2050, a substantial fraction of pharmaceutical small molecules — possibly the majority — will be produced by engineered microbes in fermenters rather than by chemical synthesis or plant extraction.
A substantial fraction of specialty chemicals — flavors, fragrances, cosmetic ingredients, food additives — will be microbially produced.
A meaningful fraction of bulk chemicals will be microbially produced, increasing year by year as the technology matures and carbon pricing makes petrochemical production more expensive.
Alt-protein and microbial food ingredients will be a normal part of the food supply rather than a niche category.
The first CO2-fed industrial fermentation processes will be operational, initially for high-value products, with steady expansion toward bulk applications.

The chemical industry of the late twenty-first century, if we are lucky and if we engineer well, will look much less like the chemical industry of the twentieth century and much more like an extension of biology — water-based, room-temperature, fed by sunlight and CO2 and waste streams, catalyzed by enzymes evolved by life and tuned by us.
That is the long arc.
Metabolic engineering is one of the most important tools we have for making it real.
Whether it succeeds depends in part on the engineering — which is going well — and in part on the policy and market context in which the engineering operates, which is more uncertain.
But the science is on a trajectory that, ten years from now, will make today's capabilities look quaint, the same way today's capabilities make 1995's look quaint.
If you are in this field, you are in it at a moment when the foundational tools are finally good enough to attempt at scale what the founders of the discipline imagined in the 1990s.
That is a remarkable position to be in.
Make the most of it.
Thank you.
Questions.
